chunks.text: David Attwood is Professor in Residence Emeritus at UC Berkeley. He received his Ph.D. in Applied Physics from New York University in 1972. He has been at UC Berkeley since 1989, co-founded the Applied Science and Technology Ph.D. program, and has been faculty advisor for the undergraduate Engineering Physics program for 25 years.
chunks.question: What is David Attwood's academic position and background?
chunks.answer: Professor Emeritus with a Ph.D. in Applied Physics.

chunks.text: His research focuses on short wavelength electromagnetic radiation, including x-rays and extreme ultraviolet radiation in the 0.1-30 nm range. Key interests are coherence at x-ray wavelengths, element-specific nanoscale imaging, and EUV lithography. His work involves Fourier optics, image contrast techniques, and developing coherent sources at these wavelengths.
chunks.question: What are David Attwood's main research areas?
chunks.answer: X-rays, extreme ultraviolet radiation, and nanoscale imaging.

chunks.text: At Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, he was founding Director of the Center for X-Ray Optics (CXRO) and first Scientific Director of the Advanced Light Source (ALS) from 1985 to 1988. He is a Fellow of the American Physical Society, Optical Society of America, and Japanese Society of Applied Physics (2016).
chunks.question: What leadership roles has David Attwood held at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory?
chunks.answer: Founding Director of CXRO and first ALS Scientific Director.

chunks.text: He authored 'X-Rays and Extreme Ultraviolet Radiation: Principles and Applications' (Cambridge University Press, 2017). His lectures are broadcast online, with recent courses including X-rays and Extreme Ultraviolet Radiation in Fall 2016, 2017, and 2019. Class materials are available at www.cambridge.org/xrayeuv.
chunks.question: What book did David Attwood author and where are his course materials?
chunks.answer: Authored a Cambridge Press book on x-rays and EUV.

chunks.text: Selected publications include works on coherence properties of x-ray free-electron laser pulses (2011), nanoscale x-ray imaging (2010), single-cycle nonlinear optics (2008), and soft x-ray microscopy achieving better than 15 nm resolution (2005). Other notable papers cover tunable coherent soft x-rays and extreme ultraviolet lithography.
chunks.question: What are some key topics in David Attwood's selected publications?
chunks.answer: X-ray coherence, nanoscale imaging, and EUV lithography.

chunks.text: Honors and awards include Fellow of the Japanese Society of Applied Physics (2016), American Physical Society Fellow (2006), and Optica Fellow (formerly Optical Society of America, 1994).
chunks.question: What fellowships has David Attwood received?
chunks.answer: Fellow of JSAP, APS, and Optica.
